dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 19 for a breathing problem located at --Address Redacted-- in [CITY].
Hello, my name is Steven, an EMT with [CITY] EMS. What brought you to call 911 today?
I can't catch my breath. It started this morning around six.
She's been on oxygen at home. Her oxygen saturation dropped earlier, so I increased the flow a bit.
Are you awake, ma'am? Can you tell me what today's date is?
It's the 23rd.
Where do you think we are?
In [CITY].
Let's check your breathing quickly. Can I listen to your lungs?
Her respirations are shallow and labored. Rate is 18 per minute.
Her SpO2 is 99% on 3 liters of oxygen.
I heard rales in both lungs. That could mean congestion or fluid in your lungs.
Pulse is 125, and BP is 179 over 102.
I'm going to give you high-flow oxygen. Just a quick moment here.
I feel a little better. My breathing feels easier now.
When did your shortness of breath begin?
She started having breathing issues when she woke up. Her color seemed off later.

What medications are you on now? 19. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: medications; Bystander: She uses Lasix, [CITY], Protonix, Coreg, and others like Atorvastatin, Spironolactone, Allopurinol, Metformin, [CITY], and Magnesium Oxide. 20. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: medications; EMT: Does she use sitagliptin as well? 21. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: medications; Bystander: Yes, it's part of her [CITY].
Any allergies you know of, ma'am? 23. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: allergies; Patient: I don't think I have any.
What's your medical history?
She was diagnosed with congestive heart failure and type 2 diabetes.
When was the last thing you had to eat or drink?
I had a light breakfast a couple of hours ago.
Anything changed right before the breathing issues began?
She wakes up like this and I think it's related to her heart failure.
You mentioned you're 79 years old, correct?
Yes, and I weigh about 57 kg.
How severe is your shortness of breath right now on a scale of 0 to 10?
Maybe an 8. Hard to breathe easily.
I noticed your skin looks normal in color, no jaundice.
Rales can still be heard in both lung fields.
Let me know if you notice any new sounds.
Oxygen saturation is still at 99%.
May I check your blood glucose? Just a quick finger prick.
Glucose reading is 0. Probably due to a dry finger or low. We'll repeat if needed.
Attaching a 4-lead EKG monitor to keep track of your heart rhythm.
Starting up the nasal cannula again at 3L/min. This should help ease your breathing.
Your symptoms align with congestive heart failure. We'll now follow the pulmonary edema protocol.
Heart rate climbed to 126, BP is now 170 over 118.
EKG just showed no acute changes-still sinus tachycardia, and GCS is 15, so consciousness is intact.
We just bumped up your oxygen flow. How are you now?
I do feel better. Breathing is more manageable.
Her saturation dropped to 88%. Switching her to a non-rebreather mask at 10L.
SpO2 is back to 100% now with 10L NRB.
We need IV access. Let's attempt the start of IV in an extremity vein.
IV is placed in your left arm. Locked for the time being.
Quick question-will the Hospice nurse be calling us anytime soon?
She's under Hospice care, and the nurse will call as soon as possible.
You said shortness of breath is an 8. Any pain elsewhere?
No chest pain either. Just the breathing.
We plan to transport her to [COMPANY_NAME] for diuresis.
Vitals look stable. Heart rate 125 and saturation 100% on high flow.
All set for transport in about five minutes.
Latest vitals: heart rate 132, BP is 161 over 94.
Let's recheck the EKG-no variations so far.
SpO2 remains at 100%. No hypoxia detected.
We were delayed for a short while waiting for the Hospice call. Now good to go.
I called the Hospice office myself to expedite the call.
We're still staying with [COMPANY_NAME] for diuresis. No changes to that.
She's secured, stable, and on 10L NRB. All interventions ready.
You were acting agitated earlier but are calmer now. Breathing easier.
Earlier we had 28 per minute, now it's 22. Breathing is slower and steadier.
Now it's 20 per minute. Better air movement than before.
Review of vitals: heart rate 125, respirations 18-22, BP steady in the mid-160s, and saturation 100%.

Bedside report to the nurse: 79-year-old female with shortness of breath since waking. Vitals: 179/102 to 161/94 BP, 18-22-20 respirations, 125-132-125 heart rate, SpO2 99-88-100% with 10L NRB. IV in place on left arm. No chest pain reported. Diagnosis: Pulmonary edema / CHF.
No chest pain reported. Just shortness of breath.
That's right. No pain at all in the chest, just this breathing issue.
Pain is at a 0. No signs of pain in her responses or exam. 73. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: medications; EMT: She's on Coreg, part of her regimen for heart failure.
She was on 3L home oxygen, and it was increased temporarily. Now 3L NC with 10L NRB added for safety.
She remains alert. SpO2 is steady on 100%. Ready for transport to [COMPANY_NAME].
All set for transport. Final vitals: 125 HR, 20 RR, 153/89 BP, and 100% SpO2.
